text: Wore the Aster Flow Legging in Deep Plum through a 90 minute hot yoga class and never once had to yank the waistband back up. The fabric has that brushed, almost buttery feel without going see-through in a deep lunge.
I like the fit of the Aster Flow Legging but after maybe eight washes the inner thighs are already pilling, which stings at 98 dollars. Cold wash, hung to dry, did everything right.
The Aster Vent Tech Tee is my go-to for July runs now. Sweated straight through a 10k in Georgia humidity and it dried on the walk home instead of clinging like my old cotton shirts.

Heads up that the White colorway of the Aster Vent Tech Tee is basically sheer once you sweat. Fine over a bra you don't mind showing, but I expected more opacity at this price and ended up returning it for the Slate.

Took the Aster Daytrip Belt Bag in Olive on a week of city walking and it held my phone, keys, cards, and a small sunscreen with room to spare. The strap adjuster actually stays put, which my last belt bag never managed. Great buy for 38 bucks.

The front pocket on the Aster Daytrip Belt Bag is too shallow to be useful, my chapstick fell out twice before I stopped trusting it. Main compartment is fine, but that little pocket is mostly decorative.

The inside of the Aster Cloud Hoodie feels like a blanket and the Oatmeal color goes with everything I own. I've worn it to the studio, on flights, and to the farmers market and it still looks new after two months.

Really soft, but the Aster Cloud Hoodie shrank about half a size after one trip through the dryer on low. My fault partly, but at 118 dollars I'd expect the care instructions to be more forgiving. Order up if you like a relaxed fit.
Got caught in a 40 minute downpour walking the dog in my Aster Storm Shell Jacket and my sweater underneath was completely dry. The Storm Blue is a nicer, deeper shade in person than on the site.

The Aster Storm Shell Jacket keeps rain out, no complaints there, but it doesn't breathe at all. Ten minutes of brisk uphill walking and I was damp from the inside instead. More of a standing-around-in-rain jacket than a hiking one.

Wore the Aster Trailhead Puffer through a Minneapolis January and it genuinely held up down to the single digits with just a base layer under it. The Navy hasn't picked up any of the dinginess my old white puffer did.

Warm as promised, but the Aster Trailhead Puffer runs slim through the shoulders. I usually take a medium and had to size up to layer a hoodie underneath. At 228 dollars I also wish the pockets zipped.

The Aster Base Merino Long Sleeve in Forest has become my default under a shell for winter trail runs. Warm when I start, doesn't turn swampy by mile three, and zero smell even after back to back wears.

I love merino but the seams on the Aster Base Merino Long Sleeve sit right on my shoulder blades and get itchy under a backpack strap. Also delicate, mine snagged on a zipper the second week and left a small pull.

The Aster Studio Sports Bra is exactly right for pilates and spin, snug enough to feel supported without the compressed-sausage effect. The Deep Plum is a great color and it hasn't faded through weekly washes since March.

Fine for yoga, but I tried running in the Aster Studio Sports Bra and it's just not enough support for anything above a C cup at a bounce. Also the removable pads fold over in the wash every single time.

Ran a half marathon in the Aster Momentum Short and had zero chafing, which almost never happens for me. The little zip pocket in the back fit my key and a gel, and the Navy still looks sharp after a full season.

The Aster Momentum Short rides up between my thighs on anything longer than a couple miles. Cute for the gym and errands, frustrating for actual running. I'm 5'7" so shorter folks may not have this issue.

I basically live in the Aster Everywhere Jogger now. Wore the Charcoal pair on a red-eye and walked straight into a casual work meeting the next morning without looking like I'd slept in them, because the fabric just doesn't wrinkle.

Comfortable, but the waistband on my Aster Everywhere Jogger rolls down whenever I sit for a while, and the drawstring doesn't fully fix it. The Oatmeal also shows every speck of coffee and dirt, which is on me but worth knowing.

The Aster Commute Tote swallows my 14 inch laptop, lunch, and gym shoes and still zips shut. The padded sleeve is real padding, not the token foam layer most totes have. Six months of daily train commutes and the Charcoal fabric shows no wear at the corners.

It's a well built bag, but 128 dollars for the Aster Commute Tote feels steep when the shoulder straps are this thin. Load it with a laptop and a water bottle and they start digging in by the end of a ten minute walk.

The Aster Peak Beanie in Charcoal kept my ears warm on 6am winter runs all season and never got that itchy wool feeling. It also doesn't leave my hair a static disaster when I pull it off, which is rare.

The Aster Peak Beanie stretched out noticeably after about a month of regular wear. Started snug, now it slides up off my ears mid-walk unless I fold the cuff twice. Nice knit, just wish it held its shape.

Been teaching barre in the Aster Lumen Legging three times a week and the high waist stays exactly where I put it through every pulse and fold. The Sky color is soft and pretty in person, less pastel than the photos suggest.

Wanted to love the Aster Lumen Legging but the White pair goes sheer the moment you squat, even a size up. For 112 dollars the light colors should hold opacity. The Heather Grey is reportedly better, but I went back to black leggings from elsewhere.

Ran eight humid miles in the Aster Vent Tight last weekend and the mesh panels behind the knees actually do something, my legs stayed dry when everything else on me was soaked. The Olive is a nice muted shade in person too.

Fair warning on the White: the Aster Vent Tight goes noticeably sheer when you squat or fold forward, so I only wear that pair with a longer top. The Charcoal is fine, but at $112 I expected the light color to be more opaque.

The Aster Slate Crop Legging hits right above my ankle at 5'4" which is exactly what I want for spring walks, and the waistband stays put without digging when I sit through a whole workday after. Got the Sky and the color hasn't dulled after maybe ten washes.

I like the fabric but the Aster Slate Crop Legging runs a full size small in my experience. I'm normally a 6 in this brand and had to exchange for an 8, and the crop length made my first pair look like capris that shrunk.

These have quietly become my everyday pair. The Deep Plum Aster Trace Legging is dark enough for the office and stretchy enough for the gym after, and the fabric has that brushed, almost buttery feel without being flimsy.

Disappointed honestly. After about two months of regular wear my Aster Trace Legging started pilling along the inner thighs, which I don't expect at $110. Comfortable, yes, but my cheaper pairs have held up better.
Wore the Aster Aurora Tight for hot yoga twice a week all June and it wicks better than anything else I own. The Clay color is gorgeous, sort of a dusty terracotta that doesn't show sweat marks much.
The Aster Aurora Tight fits great through the legs but the waistband rolls down on me every time I do anything involving a forward bend. I keep hiking it back up mid-class, which gets old fast.
For $90 the Aster Base Crop Legging punches above its price. The Heather Grey survived a full season of dog walks and spin classes with zero fading, and the crop length is perfect with sneakers.

No pockets. That's my main gripe with the Aster Base Crop Legging, I have to carry my phone in my hand on runs and it drives me nuts. Otherwise a decent legging, the Oatmeal is prettier online than in person though, a bit beige-r than I hoped.
I bought the Aster Tempo Legging in Deep Plum for a half marathon training block and they've been solid, no chafing on long runs and the side pocket actually holds my phone without bouncing.

The Sand colorway of the Aster Tempo Legging shows every single thing, sweat, water splashes, you name it. Should have gone darker. The fit itself is fine but I basically only wear these to walk the dog now.
Sweatiest summer in years and the Aster Cadence Tight has been my go-to. The fabric is thin in a good way, dries fast, and the Navy still looks brand new after weekly washes since April.

At $124 the Aster Cadence Tight is the most expensive legging I own and the stitching on the left seam started coming loose within three weeks. Aster's customer service was helpful about it, but for this price the seams shouldn't be the weak point.
Really happy with the Aster Kodiak Crop Legging for the price. The Storm Blue is a deep, saturated color, the rise is high enough to stay put on hikes, and $94 feels fair for the quality.

The Aster Kodiak Crop Legging is comfortable but runs warm for anything more intense than a walk, I overheated in a spring bootcamp class. Also the crop length hits me at an awkward mid-calf spot, and I'm average height.

Third pair of Aster leggings and the Dune is the keeper. The Aster Dune Legging in Black is fully squat-proof, the compression is snug without being restrictive, and I've worn it from December runs to June pilates with no complaints.

Good legging, tough price. The Aster Dune Legging is well made and the Sky color is lovely, but $122 is steep when it does basically the same job as pairs I've bought for thirty dollars less. I'd wait for a sale.

The Aster Ridgeline Sports Bra held everything in place on a sprint workout in 90 degree heat, which is my whole test. Removable pads that actually stay put, and the Forest color is a nice change from my drawer of black bras.

Support is fine for lifting but the band on the Aster Ridgeline Sports Bra leaves red marks after an hour, even after sizing up. Wish they'd soften that elastic because the rest of the bra is genuinely comfortable.
Finally a bra I forget I'm wearing. The Aster Ember Bra in Storm Blue has just enough support for barre and errands after, and the straps don't slide off my shoulders like most of my others do.

The pads in the Aster Ember Bra fold and bunch every single time it goes through the wash, and fishing them back into place is a ten minute ordeal. Fit is nice but this design flaw makes me reach for other bras.

Wore the Aster Solstice Longline Bra as a top on its own through a heat wave and it was perfect, the longline cut means no skin sticking to the reformer and the Charcoal doesn't show sweat. Would buy again in Slate.
If you're bigger than a C cup, know that the Aster Solstice Longline Bra is more of a light-support situation. Fine for yoga, not for running. It's a cute layer but I was hoping for more hold at $72.

I trail run in the Aster Summit Sports Bra and it's been reliable through spring mud season and summer heat both. The Clay color washed well and the crossback doesn't chafe under a pack strap, which surprised me.

The Aster Summit Sports Bra is a nightmare to get off when sweaty, the crossback design turns into a wrestling match after a hard workout. Supportive once it's on, but I've nearly dislocated a shoulder taking it off twice now.
Wore the Aster Rally Bra through a 90-degree tempo run and it actually kept up, no chafing under the band where my old bra always got me. The Forest color is darker in person, which I prefer.

The Aster Rally Bra is comfortable for yoga but I would not trust it for anything higher impact than a brisk walk, there is just not much hold. Also seventy-two dollars for this level of support feels like a stretch.
I keep reaching for the Aster Zephyr Longline Bra on studio days. The longline cut stays put in inversions and the Clay shade goes with basically everything I own, all for fifty bucks.
Heads up that the White version of the Aster Zephyr Longline Bra is noticeably sheer once it stretches over the ribcage. I had to size up and even then I only wear it under something.

The Aster Glacier Sports Bra in Deep Plum has survived probably thirty washes since April and the elastic still snaps back. Honestly shocked at the quality for fifty dollars, it breathes better than bras I paid twice as much for.

I like the fabric on the Aster Glacier Sports Bra but the removable pads fold over in the wash every single time and fishing them back into place is a whole project. Would buy again if they just sewed them in.
Bought the Aster Coastal Bra in Olive for everyday wear and it disappears under a tee, no seams showing. Comfortable enough that I forgot I had it on during a six hour travel day.
The band on the Aster Coastal Bra runs tight. I am usually a medium in this brand and had to exchange for a large, and the straps still leave marks on my shoulders by the end of a long day.
The Aster Halo Longline Bra in Oatmeal has become my hot yoga default. It wicks fast, the longline band never rolls when I bend, and at fifty dollars I went back for the Deep Plum too.
Pretty bra, but the White Aster Halo Longline Bra picked up a faint gray tinge after a couple months of regular washing even on cold. I would stick to the darker colors.
For forty-eight dollars the Aster Marin Sports Bra punches above its price. The Heather Grey has this brushed, almost buttery feel and it has held its shape through weekly spin classes since spring.
Sadly the Aster Marin Sports Bra started pilling under the arms after maybe five wears, right where the strap rubs. It is soft and the fit is fine, but at this rate it will not last the year.
Took the Aster Torrent Tee in Slate on a humid trail run last weekend and it dried before I got back to the car. It also does not cling when you sweat, which is rare for a fitted tee.
The Aster Torrent Tee fits great out of the box but mine shrank about half an inch in length after a warm dryer cycle, and now it sits shorter than I like. Hang dry it or size up.
The Aster Storm Tank in Ember Red is the first tank I have found where the armholes do not gape when I row. Nice weight to the fabric, not flimsy at all.
I wanted to love the Aster Storm Tank but the White is see-through enough that my bra color is obvious in daylight. The Slate one I bought later is fine, so it is really just a white fabric problem.

Wore the Aster Field Long Sleeve in Oatmeal on a 35-degree morning run and it was the perfect single layer, warm at the start without cooking me by mile three. The thumbholes actually line up with my wrists too, which never happens.

Nice cold-weather piece, but the seam along the shoulder of the Aster Field Long Sleeve rubs when I wear a backpack over it, and after a couple hikes I had a raw spot. Fine for running, just not for carrying anything.

The Aster Pace Training Top has become my go-to for July bootcamp sessions, the Charcoal hides sweat and the mesh back panel genuinely moves air. Fifty-six dollars felt reasonable for something I wear twice a week.
The Aster Pace Training Top runs big through the torso. I am between sizes and went with my usual medium, and it billows enough that it rides up during burpees. Would size down if I bought it again.

The Clay color on the Aster Boreal Tee is exactly what the photos show, sort of a warm terracotta, and the fabric has a substantial drape that reads nicer than a normal workout tee. I have worn it to the gym and then straight to lunch.
It is a lovely tee but eighty dollars for the Aster Boreal Tee is hard to justify when it does not do anything my thirty dollar tees do not. If it goes on sale, grab it, at full price I would pass.

I layer the Aster Alpine Tank in Slate under my shell for winter runs and it holds warmth at my core without any bulk under the arms. The fabric is thicker than a summer tank, which is exactly the point.

Ninety dollars for a tank top is a lot, and the Aster Alpine Tank confused me in practice, too warm to wear alone and an awkward layering piece since my arms freeze anyway. A long sleeve at this price would have served me better.

The Aster Crest Long Sleeve sounds odd for hot weather but the fabric is so light that I wear the Olive one for sun coverage on long August runs and stay cooler than in a tee. Washed it a dozen times, zero fading so far.

The sleeves on the Aster Crest Long Sleeve are cut slim, and if you have any arm muscle they grip like a compression top, which I did not want. At ninety dollars I expected a bit more ease in the fit.
Wore the Aster Vertex Training Top through a full HIIT class and it stayed put, no riding up when I went into planks. The Deep Plum is richer in person than the photos show.
The fabric on the Aster Vertex Training Top gets noticeably see-through when you stretch, so I have to be careful what bra I wear under it. Fine for lifting, not something I'd wear for yoga.
The Aster Horizon Tee is heavier than a normal tee, which is exactly what I wanted for cold morning walks with a vest over it. The Clay color goes with everything I own.
I like the weight of the Aster Horizon Tee but 84 dollars for a t-shirt is hard to justify, and mine started pilling under the arms after maybe five washes. Expected better at this price.
Ran six miles in 90 degree heat in the Aster Cedar Tank and it never got that soggy clingy feeling. I got the Sand and immediately ordered the Olive too.
The armholes on the Aster Cedar Tank are cut really deep, so my sports bra shows from the side no matter what. It breathes great but I only wear it around the house now.
Got caught in a solid twenty-minute downpour walking the dog and the Aster Cloud Jacket didn't let a drop through, and my shirt underneath wasn't clammy either. The Forest colorway is gorgeous.
The Aster Cloud Jacket keeps rain out, no complaints there, but the hood is shallow and slides back off my head unless I cinch it uncomfortably tight. Annoying flaw on a 180 dollar rain jacket.

The Aster Nimbus Shell blocks wind way better than I expected for how light it packs down. I sized up to layer a fleece under it for winter hikes and the fit through the shoulders is still trim, not boxy.

My Aster Nimbus Shell in Clay picks up marks like crazy, and the zipper pull is so small I can't work it with gloves on, which matters for a cold-weather shell. Warm enough, just some frustrating details.

I stood through my kid's entire outdoor hockey game in the Aster Drift Puffer, maybe 20 degrees out, and I was actually comfortable. The Charcoal doesn't show dirt and the pockets are fleece-lined, small thing but I notice it every day.

Feathers work their way out of the seams of the Aster Drift Puffer constantly. I'm picking down off my sweaters every time I wear it, and at 254 dollars that shouldn't be happening in the first winter.
The Aspen Parka hits mid-thigh on me at 5'6" so it actually covers where I want it to. I've been wearing the Aster Aspen Parka in Oatmeal all fall and it still looks new after a wash.

The Aster Aspen Parka is a fine mild-weather coat, but the sleeves run long, past my knuckles, and there are no cuff adjusters to fix it. I'd size down if I could do it again, though then it might be snug through the hips.

I run in the rain twice a week and the Aster Meridian Windbreaker has become my default, light enough that I forget it's on but it keeps the drizzle off through a full 10k. The Navy hasn't faded at all.

254 dollars is a lot for a windbreaker and the Aster Meridian Windbreaker doesn't quite earn it. In steady rain, water eventually creeps through the shoulder seams, so it's more of a drizzle jacket than an actual rain layer.

Took the Aster Trailhead Jacket up a windy ridge in November and it shut the cold out completely without making me sweat on the climb. The Slate color with the two-way zip, they got the details right on this one.

The Aster Trailhead Jacket runs small through the chest and shoulders, my usual medium was tight the second I layered anything under it. Had to return for a large, so factor the exchange hassle into your order.

The Aster Frost Shell got me through a week of single-digit mornings at the bus stop. Storm Blue is a nice change from every black coat I own, and the collar zips high enough that I skip a scarf most days.
Warm, yes, but the Aster Frost Shell crinkles loudly every time I move my arms, like walking around in a chip bag. Also the hand pockets sit oddly high. I keep it for shoveling snow, not much else.
The Aster Flow Puffer is that perfect in-between weight for 40-50 degree days when a real winter coat is too much. Mine is the Deep Plum and I get asked about it constantly.

I love how the Aster Flow Puffer looks but it lost noticeable loft after one gentle machine wash, even following the care tag exactly. For 250 dollars I expected it to survive laundry better than that.

Commuted through a genuinely nasty week of rain in the Aster Lumen Parka and stayed dry from hood to hem, laptop bag under the coat and all. The Black version reads polished enough for the office too.

The Aster Lumen Parka does its job in the rain, but the waterproof coating means zero breathability, so I'm damp from the inside after a brisk fifteen-minute walk. Great for standing around, sweaty for actually moving.

The Aster Vent Windbreaker cuts the wind on cold morning runs without turning into a sauna by mile two, the back vents actually work. Got the Deep Plum, sits close through the waist without being tight.

The elastic cuffs on the Aster Vent Windbreaker dig into my wrists hard enough to leave marks, and the pockets are too shallow to trust with my phone. Blocks wind fine, but I reach for a different jacket most days.

Got the Aster Slate Hoodie in Deep Plum and the brushed inside is soft without being that fake-plush fleece that mats down. I've worn it to the studio and back three times a week since April and it still looks new out of the wash.
The fabric on the Aster Slate Hoodie is lovely but $138 for a hoodie with a kangaroo pocket this shallow feels like a stretch. My phone slides half out every time I sit down.

I throw the Aster Trace Zip Hoodie on over a tank for early cold-morning dog walks and it actually blocks the chill. The zipper is smooth and doesn't snag the way my old one did. Navy is a true dark navy, not washed out.

Disappointed with the white Aster Trace Zip Hoodie. After maybe five washes the cuffs went dingy and the fabric started pilling under the arms where my bag strap rubs. Fine for the price, but I expected it to hold up longer.

The Aurora Pullover in Oatmeal has become my post-run layer. Sized my normal medium and the sleeves are actually long enough to thumb-hole over my hands, which almost never happens for me. The Aster Aurora Pullover was worth the wait on backorder.

Heads up that the Aster Aurora Pullover runs roomy. I usually take a small and this fits more like an oversized medium, and the neckline is wide enough that it slips off one shoulder. Cute look if you want that, but it wasn't what I ordered it for.
Wore the Aster Base Hoodie in Forest through a windy 40-degree weekend camping and stayed genuinely warm without a jacket over it. Heavier than it looks in photos, in a good way.
I like the Aster Base Hoodie but the Deep Plum bled in the first wash and tinted a pair of grey socks. Wash it alone. Also the hood is on the small side if you have a lot of hair.

The Sky color on the Aster Tempo Zip Hoodie is even prettier in person, sort of a soft chambray blue. Light enough for spring evenings, and the pockets zip, so my keys survived a full flow class and the walk home.

Ordered the Aster Tempo Zip Hoodie in White and it is thinner than I expected, borderline see-through over a dark sports bra. Returned it. If you want an actual hoodie and not a glorified layer, look elsewhere.

Third cold season with hoodies from this brand and the Aster Cadence Pullover in Storm Blue might be the best one. The inside stays fuzzy after washing and the ribbed hem doesn't flip up when I raise my arms.

Nice weight, but the sleeves on the Aster Cadence Pullover are weirdly snug through the forearm compared to the rest of it, which fits true. If you lift at all, size up. Also $132 stings a little for a basic pullover.

Honestly surprised the Aster Kodiak Hoodie is only $98 next to the rest of the line. The Clay color goes with everything I own and it kept its shape after I accidentally put it through the dryer twice.

The Aster Kodiak Hoodie is comfortable but the drawstrings came out in the wash and rethreading them took me twenty minutes with a safety pin. Small thing, just annoying. The Charcoal also shows lint like crazy.

I run cold and the Aster Dune Zip Hoodie in Ember Red got me through my kids' November soccer games without adding a coat. The high collar zips all the way up over my chin, which I didn't know I needed.

The Sand colorway of the Aster Dune Zip Hoodie shows every coffee drip and mark, which is on me, but the bigger issue is the main zipper catches on the interior fabric near the bottom almost every time. You learn to work around it, but at $130 you shouldn't have to.

Perfect mild-weather layer. I hiked four miles in the Aster Ridgeline Pullover last Saturday and never overheated, and the Sky color hasn't faded even though I've washed it weekly. My mom already asked me to order her one.

The seams inside the shoulders of the Aster Ridgeline Pullover are raised and scratchy against bare skin. Fine over a long sleeve, irritating over a tank, which is exactly how I planned to wear it. Sent mine back.
Bought the Aster Ember Hoodie in Slate for winter commutes and it's dense enough that wind doesn't cut through it at the bus stop. A hundred dollars felt fair for how substantial it is.

After a couple months the Navy on my Aster Ember Hoodie faded noticeably at the shoulders, I assume from sun on daily walks. Still warm, still comfortable, just doesn't look as sharp as the day it arrived.
Ran a sweaty 10k in 85-degree heat in the Aster Solstice Short and had zero chafing, which is rare for my thighs. The Storm Blue dries fast enough that I wore them straight to brunch after.
Cute shorts, but the waistband on the Aster Solstice Short rolls down when I bend or squat, and the Oatmeal is a bit sheer when stretched. I keep them for casual wear rather than actual workouts now.

The liner in the Aster Summit Lined Short actually stays put instead of riding up, which is the whole battle with lined shorts. Wore the Olive pair for a trail run and the little zip pocket held my key without bouncing.

These run small. I ordered the Aster Summit Lined Short in my usual size and the liner was uncomfortably tight through the hips while the outer shell fit fine, so sizing up would just make the shell baggy. Awkward fit for anyone remotely curvy.

I squat-tested the Aster Rally Bike Short in Navy under gym lights and they are fully opaque, which is more than I can say for shorts twice this price. No rolling, no digging, and they stayed in place through a full hot yoga class.

The Heather Grey Aster Rally Bike Short shows sweat marks within ten minutes of any real workout, so I only wear them for errands. Fabric itself is nice, but grey bike shorts and summer heat are a bad combo, wish I'd gone with the Navy.
The Aster Zephyr Short in Slate has become my default for tempo days when the weather sits around 60 degrees. Liner doesn't ride up, which is honestly all I ask of a running short.
I like the fabric but the pockets on the Aster Zephyr Short are so shallow my phone worked its way out twice on one jog. For $72 I expected a zip pocket that could actually hold something.
Wore the Aster Glacier Lined Short through a humid 90-degree week in July and the liner wicked better than any pair I own. The Clay color is more of a warm terracotta in person, which I love.
The waistband on the Aster Glacier Lined Short rolls down every single time I bend over in yoga or stretch after a run. Frustrating at eighty dollars.
I've done maybe fifteen spin classes in the Aster Coastal Bike Short and the leg hems still haven't budged or flipped. Got the Storm Blue and it hasn't faded in the wash either.
Fair warning that the Aster Coastal Bike Short runs small. I'm usually a medium in this brand and had to exchange for a large, and even that is snug through the hips.
Bought the Aster Halo Short in Oatmeal for hot-weather walks and it's featherlight without feeling flimsy. True to size for me, hits mid-thigh, doesn't bunch.
Love the cut of the Aster Halo Short but the Oatmeal is noticeably see-through when it's sweaty or stretched. Should have gone with the Black.
The Aster Marin Lined Short got me through a half marathon training block with zero chafing, which I did not expect at $68. The Ember Red is bold in the best way.
After about ten washes the liner in my Aster Marin Lined Short started pilling and feels scratchy against the skin now. The outer shell still looks fine, so it's a shame the inside gave out first.
For $58 the Aster Torrent Bike Short punches way above its price. The Deep Plum is squat-proof, the fabric feels buttery, and it stayed put through a 95-degree trail hike.
The Aster Torrent Bike Short kept sliding down at the waist on my run, so I spent three miles hiking them up. The drawcord helps a little but I shouldn't need to cinch them that hard.
Grabbed the Aster Storm Short in Navy for spring runs and it hits that in-between weight perfectly, not clammy, not paper thin. Waistband sits flat under a hip pack too.
My Clay pair of the Aster Storm Short faded to a dull pinkish tone after a couple months of regular washing on cold. Fit is still great, the color just didn't hold.

I basically live in the Aster Field Jogger from the school run to the office to evening walks. The Heather Grey hides everything, and the tapered ankle actually stays put over sneakers instead of creeping up.
Comfortable jogger, but $100 for the Aster Field Jogger feels steep when the drawstring tips frayed within the first month. I've had cheaper pairs hold up better.
Ran through most of January in the Aster Pace Pant and my legs stayed warm without overheating on the uphill stretches. The Charcoal brushed interior is genuinely cozy.
The White colorway of the Aster Pace Pant is lovely but slightly sheer over dark underwear, which is not what you want in a $124 winter pant. Sticking to indoor wear with these.
The Aster Boreal Track Pant in Deep Plum has been my travel pant all spring. Wore it on a red-eye, hiked in it the next morning, and it shook out wrinkle-free both times.
At $138 I wanted to love the Aster Boreal Track Pant, but the ankle zips scratch against my heel every time I walk. Quality fabric, annoying hardware.
The Sand color of the Aster Alpine Jogger goes with everything I own, and the fabric has a nice matte finish that doesn't look like gym wear. Held its shape after a dozen washes so far.
The Aster Alpine Jogger runs long. I'm 5'4" and even after hemming there's fabric pooling at my ankles, so shorter women should know there's no petite length offered.
Winter dog walks in the Aster Crest Pant have been a game changer, the fleece backing blocks wind down to freezing without feeling bulky. The Forest green is a nice break from all my black leggings.
The Aster Crest Pant kept me warm but the inseam seams itch against my thighs once I start sweating. Fine for errands, not great for an actual workout.
I wear the Aster Vertex Track Pant year round, light enough for a cool summer evening, layers over tights in winter. The Sky color reads soft blue-grey in person and I get compliments constantly.
The side pockets on the Aster Vertex Track Pant are too shallow to trust with keys, and for $134 that's a real miss. Otherwise a well-made pant, which makes the pocket thing more irritating.

Grabbed the Aster Horizon Jogger in Deep Plum for travel days and they've basically lived on me since. The taper hits right at the ankle so they don't bunch over sneakers, and the fabric stays smooth even after being crammed in a carry-on.
The Aster Horizon Jogger looks great but the pockets are shallow enough that my phone worked its way out twice on the couch. For $116 I expected pockets I could actually trust on a walk.
I wore the Aster Cedar Pant on early morning dog walks all through February and my legs never got that windy-cold sting. The Olive is a nice muted shade that reads more workwear than gym.
After maybe eight washes the inner thighs of my Aster Cedar Pant started pilling where they rub. Still warm, still comfortable, but they look older than three months should.

The Aster Cloud Track Pant in Oatmeal is the first light-colored pant I own that isn't see-through when I squat down to tie my shoes. Brushed on the inside, structured on the outside, and I've worn them from a chilly morning class straight into a warm afternoon without overheating.
Runs big. I sized down in the Aster Cloud Track Pant per my usual and the waistband still slides when the drawcord loosens mid-run, so I'm constantly hiking them up.
These fit like they were cut for someone with actual hips, which is rare. The Aster Nimbus Jogger in Forest has become my post-yoga default and the cuffs haven't stretched out even with weekly wear.
$126 for the Aster Nimbus Jogger feels steep when the drawstring frayed at the tips within the first month. Lovely fabric, but at this price the details should hold up better.
Ran 10k in 90-degree humidity in the Aster Drift Tee and it never got that heavy soaked-shirt feel. The Sand color also hides sweat marks way better than my grey shirts do.
Solid hot-weather shirt but the Ember Red in my Aster Drift Tee faded noticeably after a summer of washing, more brick than ember now. I'd buy Navy next time and skip the bright colors.
The Aster Aspen Tank in Charcoal sits close without clinging, which is exactly what I want on pull day. Armholes are cut high enough that it doesn't gape when I'm on the rings.
The seam along the shoulder of the Aster Aspen Tank rubbed a raw spot during a long rowing session. Fine for lifting, but I wouldn't wear it for anything repetitive.

Been running in the Aster Meridian Long Sleeve on 35-degree mornings and it's the perfect first layer, warm at the start, doesn't cook you by mile three. Thumbholes actually line up with my wrists for once.
Good shirt, but the White version of the Aster Meridian Long Sleeve is sheer enough that you can see a base layer through it clearly. Should have gone Slate.

The Aster Trailhead Training Top handled a full August trail race without chafing anywhere, and the mesh panels under the arms actually move air instead of just looking technical. Black still looks new after a season.
I found the Aster Trailhead Training Top runs a full size small, the medium fit me like a compression shirt across the chest. Had to return and reorder, so check the size chart before you buy.
Soft is an understatement. My Aster Frost Tee in Olive has been through the wash a dozen times and hasn't shrunk or twisted at the hem, which is more than I can say for shirts twice the price.
Seventy dollars is a lot for the Aster Frost Tee when the fabric is thin enough to wrinkle badly in a gym bag. It feels premium on but the price-to-shirt ratio is off for me.

I layer the Aster Flow Tank under a hoodie for cold garage workouts and it wicks well enough that I'm not clammy when I strip the layers off. The Clay color is a nice change from the usual black-and-grey rotation.
A tank rated for cold weather is a weird sell, and on its own the Aster Flow Tank left my arms freezing walking to the gym. It's really a layering piece, the listing should say so.

Wore the Aster Lumen Long Sleeve for sun coverage on a July hike and it stayed cooler than going sleeveless, honestly. The Slate doesn't show dust or sweat, which matters when you're eight miles from the trailhead.
The cuffs on my Aster Lumen Long Sleeve stretched out after a few weeks and now flop around my wrists. Great fabric for hot days, but it's losing its shape faster than I'd like.
The Aster Vent Training Top in Forest breathes exactly where it should, my back stopped soaking through during circuit days. Fit is athletic without being tight around the middle.
The zipper pull on the Aster Vent Training Top rattles when I run and it drives me a little crazy on quiet mornings. Also snagged the fabric near the collar once already.

Didn't expect a tee this warm. The Aster Slate Tee has a dense, almost merino-like feel and worked as my only top on a brisk 50-degree run. Sky is a great color in person, less pastel than the photos.

$88 for a t-shirt is a stretch, and my Aster Slate Tee developed a small hole at the hem seam after two months of normal wear. Aster made it right with a replacement, but the price should buy better stitching.
Wore the Aster Trace Tank through a string of 90 degree tempo runs and it never once clung to my back. The charcoal hides sweat marks way better than my old grey tanks did.
Decent tank but the armholes on the Aster Trace Tank are cut huge, so it flaps around and shows a lot of side when I'm on the rower. If you're between sizes, definitely go down.
Got caught in a proper downpour on my bike commute and the Aster Aurora Jacket kept everything under it bone dry. The storm blue is a nicer, deeper shade in person than the photos suggest.
The Aster Aurora Jacket sheds rain fine, but there's zero ventilation, so after ten minutes of walking uphill I was soaked from the inside instead. Pit zips would fix this at $166.

I've worn the Aster Base Shell over a fleece for early morning dog walks all through a cold spring and the wind just doesn't get through. Sleeves are long enough that they stay put when I reach up, which is rare for me.
For $224 I expected better pockets on the Aster Base Shell. They're so shallow my phone worked its way out twice, once onto pavement. Warm jacket, careless details.
Shoveled the driveway in the Aster Tempo Puffer at 15 degrees and had to unzip halfway through because I was too warm. The navy still looks new after a full winter of daily wear.
Warm, sure, but the Aster Tempo Puffer runs at least a half size large through the body. My usual medium looks like I borrowed it, and by the time I noticed, returns had closed.

The Aster Cadence Parka has become my default for chilly-but-not-cold mornings. It's long enough to cover my seat on a damp bench at my kid's games, and the slate color goes with basically everything I own.

Bought the white Aster Cadence Parka and regretted it within a month. Every strap, seatbelt, and doorframe left a mark, and it's spot-clean only, so it looks tired already. Should not sell this color without saying that.
Three weeks of Pacific Northwest drizzle and the Aster Kodiak Windbreaker hasn't wetted out once. It packs down small enough to live in my work bag, which is really why I bought it.
The hood on the Aster Kodiak Windbreaker has no adjustment cord, so the first real gust blew it straight off my head and the rain went down my collar. Odd miss on a $208 rain jacket.

The ember red Aster Dune Jacket gets compliments every time I wear it, and more importantly it kept me comfortable standing around at a 30 degree tailgate for four hours. Zipper glides one-handed with gloves on.
I like the Aster Dune Jacket, I just can't tell you it's worth $266. My friend's jacket at half the price is nearly as warm, and after one season the cuffs are already fraying a little.
Skied a full week in the Aster Ridgeline Shell over a midlayer and never felt the wind on the lifts, even on the exposed ridge runs. The storm blue looks sharp against snow in photos too.
The Aster Ridgeline Shell is genuinely windproof but the fabric is loud, that crinkly swish-swish with every step. Fine on a mountain, drove me a little crazy walking around town in it.
I run cold and the Aster Ember Puffer in charcoal is the first jacket I actually unzip on my walk to the train. Light enough that I forget I'm wearing it, which sounds like nothing but isn't.

After maybe fifteen wears the Aster Ember Puffer started leaking down through the seams. Little feathers on every sweater I layer under it. At $264 that's not acceptable, and mild days are exactly when it happens because I'm carrying it around.

Wore the navy Aster Solstice Parka through a soggy weekend of youth soccer and stayed completely dry, sidelines to parking lot. The longer cut means my thighs don't get soaked like they did in my old rain jacket.

The Aster Solstice Parka does its job in the rain, but the sizing runs slim through the shoulders. I couldn't get a sweater under my usual large without feeling like a sausage, so I sized up and now the sleeves are too long.

Been running in the Aster Summit Windbreaker on 35 degree mornings and it blocks the wind without turning into a sweat lodge by mile three. The deep plum is a nice change from every jacket being black.

Solid enough windbreaker, but the elastic cuffs on the Aster Summit Windbreaker are tight to the point of leaving marks on my wrists. I keep pushing the sleeves up to get relief, which defeats the purpose in the cold.
The inside of the Aster Rally Hoodie is the softest fleece I've owned, and the olive color survived probably twenty washes without going flat. I reach for it over hoodies that cost more.
Comfortable hoodie, but the Aster Rally Hoodie has no front pocket depth to speak of. My keys and phone barely sit in there and anything else falls out the second I slouch on the couch.

I wear the Aster Zephyr Zip Hoodie in heather grey as my warm-up layer at the gym and as my actual sweatshirt everywhere else. The zipper hasn't snagged once in six months, which my last two hoodies could not claim.

The sand Aster Zephyr Zip Hoodie pilled badly under the arms and where my backpack straps sit, and I'm only maybe ten washes in, all cold and hung dry. Fit and warmth are fine, the fabric just isn't holding up.

Grabbed the Aster Glacier Pullover in Oatmeal and it's become my default for cool morning dog walks. The interior brushing is soft without being that cheap fleecy stuff that pills, and the cuffs actually stay put when I push the sleeves up.

The fabric on the Aster Glacier Pullover is genuinely nice, but at $128 I expected the kangaroo pocket to be deeper than it is. My phone half slides out every time I sit down. Fine for standing around, annoying otherwise.

I wore the Aster Coastal Hoodie in Olive through most of a cold, windy weekend camping trip and never reached for my jacket. Heavier than it looks in photos, in a good way. Sizing was true for me at a large.

Decent hoodie, but my Ember Red Aster Coastal Hoodie noticeably faded after maybe six or seven washes, cold water and all. It's more of a dusty brick color now. If you're buying it for the red, temper your expectations.

The zipper on the Aster Halo Zip Hoodie is the smoothest I've had on any midlayer, no snagging on the storm flap. I've been wearing the Sand one to the gym and back for two months and it still looks new out of the dryer.

I wanted to love the Aster Halo Zip Hoodie but $136 for a hoodie where the collar seam rubs the back of my neck is a hard sell. Broke in slightly after a few wears, never fully went away. Returning mine.

Really solid cold-weather layer. The Aster Marin Pullover in Forest kept me warm shoveling the driveway in the low 30s with just a t-shirt under it, and the extended collar is great when you don't want the hood up.

Heads up that the Aster Marin Pullover runs slim through the shoulders. I'm a pretty standard medium in everything else and this one felt restrictive reaching overhead. Exchanged for a large and it's fine, but the sleeves are now a touch long.

The Aster Torrent Hoodie hits that weight where you can wear it on a 55 degree run and not overheat, then throw it back on after. The Clay color is nicer in person than online. Zero complaints after a couple months.

My black Aster Torrent Hoodie started pilling under the arms and where my backpack straps sit after about a month of regular wear. Still comfortable, but for $116 I expected the face fabric to hold up better.

Bought the Aster Storm Zip Hoodie in Charcoal for winter commuting and it's done the job better than jackets twice the price. Dense knit, blocks wind surprisingly well, and the hood actually fits over a beanie.

Warm and well made, but the hand pockets on the Aster Storm Zip Hoodie are shallow enough that my keys have fallen out twice getting out of the car. A small zip pocket would fix it. Otherwise I like it a lot.

The Storm Blue on the Aster Field Pullover is a great muted shade that goes with everything I own. Fabric has a nice structured feel, doesn't bag out at the elbows the way my old pullovers did, and it's held its shape through a dozen washes.

It's a nice piece, but I struggle to see where the $132 goes on the Aster Field Pullover. No thumbholes, no zip pocket, just a well-cut basic pullover. If it dips on sale I'd say grab it, at full price it feels steep.

I run cold and the Aster Pace Hoodie has been my go-to for early winter runs, warm at the start without turning into a sauna by mile three. Got the Olive and the fit through the chest is athletic without being tight.

The body of the Aster Pace Hoodie is great, but the ribbed hem is aggressive and rides up over my hips instead of sitting flat. I'm constantly tugging it down. Might just be my build, but worth knowing before you order.

Took the Aster Boreal Short in Sky on a beach trip in 95 degree humidity and they dried fast enough to wear straight from the water to dinner. Light, airy, and the drawcord doesn't come untied mid-run either.

The fabric on the Aster Boreal Short is so lightweight that the Sand colorway gets a little see-through when it's wet or stretched. Fine over a liner or swimsuit, but I wouldn't wear them commando to the gym.

The liner in the Aster Alpine Lined Short is the reason I bought a second pair. Supportive without that compression-short squeeze, and no chafing on a 6 mile run. Heather Grey hides sweat better than I expected too.

I like the Aster Alpine Lined Short but the liner is warm. On anything above 75 degrees my legs are cooking by the end of a workout. These are shoulder-season shorts, not summer shorts, whatever the product photos suggest.

Did a 30 mile ride in July heat in the Aster Crest Bike Short and the leg grippers stayed put the whole time, no bunching behind the knee. The Forest color is sharp and the chamois placement actually makes sense.

The waistband on my Aster Crest Bike Short rolls down every time I get into the drops, and I have to keep fixing it mid-ride. Sized correctly per the chart. For $80 that's a dealbreaker for me on a bike short.
At $58 the Aster Vertex Short punches above its price. I've been rotating the Navy pair through gym sessions and errands for two months, and the fabric still looks crisp with no fraying at the hems.

Fair warning, the Aster Vertex Short runs about a size large. My usual medium sat low on my hips even with the drawstring cinched all the way. The small fits right, but that guessing game cost me a return shipment.

The Aster Horizon Lined Short in Storm Blue got me through a week of 90 degree trail runs. The liner wicks fast, the outer shell never clings, and the zip pocket holds my phone without it slapping my thigh.

After about ten washes the liner seams in my Aster Horizon Lined Short have gotten scratchy along the inner thigh, enough that I feel it on longer runs. Great short for the first couple months, so it's the durability that knocks off stars.

Picked up the Aster Cedar Bike Short in Deep Plum and it's become my default for spring gym sessions. The fabric has just enough compression to stay put during lunges without feeling like a sausage casing.

The Aster Cedar Bike Short fits well, but heads up on the Oatmeal colorway: it gets noticeably see-through in a deep squat. I'd stick to the darker option, and honestly at 80 bucks I expected them to have solved that.
Wore the Aster Cloud Short through a humid 90-degree week in Austin and it earned the name. Dries fast, doesn't cling, and the Navy hides sweat better than my old pair ever did.
Comfortable short, but the pockets on the Aster Cloud Short are shallow enough that my phone bounced out twice on a run. Fine for the gym, wouldn't trust it with keys on trails.
The liner in the Aster Nimbus Lined Short is the best I've tried, no chafing on a 10k and it doesn't ride up. Got the Forest and it looks sharp enough to wear to brunch after.
I wanted to love the Aster Nimbus Lined Short but the liner is cut short and dug into my inner thigh by mile three. Sized up and it just got baggy in the waist instead. Returning.

Been cycling to work in the Aster Drift Bike Short all June. The Charcoal doesn't show road grime and after a dozen washes the leg grippers still hold, which is more than I can say for pricier brands.
The waistband on the Aster Drift Bike Short rolls down when I'm bent over the handlebars for more than twenty minutes. Great fabric, annoying design flaw for a short clearly aimed at riders.
Grabbed the Aster Aspen Short in Storm Blue for a hiking trip and it handled scrambles and creek crossings without complaint. The fabric has a nice matte finish, doesn't look technical.
My Ember Red Aster Aspen Short faded to more of a salmon after maybe eight washes, cold water and everything. Fit and feel are genuinely good, so it's frustrating the color couldn't keep up.
The Aster Meridian Jogger in Clay is the first jogger I've owned that tapers right at the ankle without choking my calves. I travel weekly and these have basically replaced my sweats and my chinos.

Nice pant, but $128 is a lot for the Aster Meridian Jogger when the drawstring frays at the tips after a couple months and the back pocket has no closure. The fabric quality is there, the finishing details aren't.
Took the Aster Trailhead Pant on a cold-weather backpacking trip in the Charcoal and stayed warm down to about 35 degrees moving. The articulated knees actually work on steep climbs.

The Aster Trailhead Pant runs big. I'm a solid medium in everything Aster makes and these hang off my hips even with the belt loops cinched. The brushed interior also pills where my pack's hipbelt rubs.
I run in the Aster Frost Track Pant year round, the Forest colorway, and the zippered ankles make it easy to pull over trainers at the track. Warm in January, somehow not swampy in May.

For $138 I expected the Aster Frost Track Pant to nail the basics, but the side pockets are so shallow my phone sticks out an inch and slaps my thigh with every stride. Good pant otherwise, bad pockets.
The Heather Grey Aster Flow Jogger has that broken-in feel straight out of the box. I've washed mine weekly since March and the cuffs haven't stretched out, which is where joggers usually die on me.

Comfortable, but the Aster Flow Jogger is cut slim through the thigh. If you squat or bike at all, size up, because true to size felt like it was one lunge away from a seam test. Wish they'd say so on the size chart.
Wore the Aster Lumen Pant in Deep Plum for early morning winter walks with the dog and the wind just does not get through. Warm without the puffy look, and the color reads almost black indoors.

The Aster Lumen Pant does its job in the cold but the inner seam along the crotch itches against bare skin, enough that I wear a base layer under it every time. At $134 the fabric touching my leg shouldn't be the weak point.

The vents on the Aster Vent Track Pant actually vent. Wore the Slate pair from a chilly morning warmup into a sweaty noon session and never felt the need to change. Rare for an all-season claim to hold up.

Decent pant, but the ankle zippers on my Aster Vent Track Pant snag the mesh lining about every third use. I've already had to work one free with tweezers. Feels like a detail that shouldn't have made it past testing.
Confession: I bought the Aster Slate Jogger in White expecting to ruin it in a month, and after coffee spills and a dozen washes it still looks new. Whatever finish they put on this fabric, it works.
$138 for the Aster Slate Jogger stings when the waistband elastic went wavy after two months of normal wear. My cheaper joggers have outlasted it. Love the Sky color, can't recommend at this price.
The Aster Trace Pant in Charcoal has been my winter commute pant since November. Blocks the wind on the bike, and at $106 it undercuts most of what I compared it against without feeling cheap.

Solid cold-weather pant, but the Heather Grey Aster Trace Pant pills along the inner thighs after a season of regular wear. It still functions fine, it just looks tired way sooner than the fabric price suggests it should.

Wore the Aster Aurora Track Pant on a cold morning walk to the office and again for evening drills and the fabric handled both without feeling clammy. The Storm Blue is darker in person, which I actually prefer.

The ankle cuffs on the Aster Aurora Track Pant are tighter than the product photos suggest, and getting them over my heels is a small wrestling match every time. Great pant otherwise, but at $130 that detail bugs me.
The Aster Base Jogger in Ember Red has become my default for warmups. Waistband sits flat under a shell and the taper is clean without being skinny.

I went with White and regretted it, the Aster Base Jogger is noticeably see-through in bright light when the fabric stretches over the knee. For $126 I expected a denser knit. Sizing was fine, just pick a darker color.

Honestly shocked the Aster Tempo Backpack is only $38. I've stuffed it with a laptop, lunch, and gym shoes daily for two months and the zippers still run smooth. Got the Olive and the color hasn't dulled.
The straps on the Aster Tempo Backpack have almost no padding, so anything over ten pounds starts digging into my shoulders on a longer walk. Fine as a light commuter bag, just know its limits.

Took the Aster Cadence Duffel as a carry-on last weekend and it swallowed three days of clothes plus a shoe compartment's worth of sneakers. The Deep Plum reads more sophisticated than sporty, which my wife and I both liked.
My Oatmeal Aster Cadence Duffel picked up scuffs from the gym floor within two weeks and they don't wipe off. Well made bag, but light colors and $112 is a rough combination.

The Aster Kodiak Sling in Navy has ridden across my chest on bike commutes all spring. Strap adjusts one-handed, main pocket fits a water bottle and a light layer, and the buckle hasn't loosened once.

I like the Aster Kodiak Sling but the front pocket is shallower than it looks, my phone sticks out the top and I've caught it mid-fall twice. Nearly a hundred dollars for a sling, the pockets should be sorted.

I take the Aster Dune Tote to yoga and then straight to errands and it doesn't look like a gym bag, which is the whole point. The Forest colorway is gorgeous and the interior sleeve actually fits a full-size mat towel and a 13 inch laptop.

No zipper on the main opening of the Aster Dune Tote, so everything is on display and once it rained into the bag while I ran for a bus. Lovely materials, but $118 for a tote that can't close feels like an oversight.
Wore the Aster Ridgeline Belt Bag on a 10k and it did not bounce once, keys, phone, and two gels sat flat against my back the whole race. Black hides sweat marks too. Easy $42.

The strap webbing on my Aster Ridgeline Belt Bag frays where it feeds through the buckle, little fuzzy threads after maybe a month of daily wear. Still functional, just doesn't look as sharp as when it arrived.

Got the Aster Ember Backpack in Heather Grey for campus and it hits a nice middle, big enough for books and a lock, small enough that I don't clip people on the train. The fabric feels sturdier than $54 usually gets you.

The Oatmeal Aster Ember Backpack shows every mark, mine has a grey smudge across the front panel from one subway ride that spot cleaning won't lift. Also wish it had a water bottle sleeve on the outside instead of inside.

The Aster Solstice Duffel has done four flights and a wet parking lot and still looks new. Deep Plum was the right call, the ventilated end pocket keeps post-run shoes away from everything else, and the shoulder pad actually stays put.

Genuinely nice bag, but $150 for the Aster Solstice Duffel is steep when the internal organization is basically one big cavity and a single slip pocket. I ended up buying packing cubes to make it work, which felt like paying twice.
I hike with the Aster Summit Sling in Slate and the back panel doesn't get swampy the way my old sling did. Fits a rain layer, snacks, and a small camera with room left over.

At $126 the Aster Summit Sling should not have a main zipper that snags on the interior lining, but mine catches at the same corner every single time. I've started opening it slowly like I'm defusing something. Considering a return.
The Aster Rally Tote in Olive goes from gym to grocery run without me thinking about it. Handles are long enough to shoulder over a winter coat, and the base has kept its shape even loaded with cans.

The interior of the Aster Rally Tote is a black hole, one small pocket for everything and my keys vanish into the bottom daily. It's a $124 tote, a key clip or a second pocket would have cost them nothing.

Took the Aster Zephyr Belt Bag in Sky on vacation and wore it crossbody every day, passport, phone, and sunscreen stick all fit with the zipper closing easily. Strap didn't chafe my neck even in the heat.

White was a mistake with the Aster Zephyr Belt Bag, it sits against your clothes and picks up dye transfer from dark jeans that will not wash out. Also $62 feels high when their other belt bag is twenty dollars less and holds nearly the same.

Ran in the Aster Glacier Beanie through most of February and my ears stayed warm without my head overheating by mile three. The Ember Red is bright enough that drivers actually see me at dawn, which I count as a feature.
The Aster Glacier Beanie fits my head fine but lost some of its snugness after two trips through the wash, now it creeps up off my ears mid-walk. Warm and soft, just wish the rib held its stretch.
Wore the Aster Coastal Cap in Sky through a whole week at the beach and it never felt sweaty or heavy. The brim keeps its shape even after being stuffed in a tote bag twice.
The Aster Coastal Cap fits my husband fine but sits big on me even with the strap cinched all the way. For a one-size unisex cap I expected a bit more range in the adjustment.
These are the socks I reach for on cold morning runs now. The Aster Halo Socks in Storm Blue kept my toes warm at 25 degrees without making my shoes feel tight, and the cuff stays up the entire time.
Comfortable, no argument there, but 32 dollars for one pair of socks is hard to justify. My Heather Grey Aster Halo Socks also started pilling at the heel after maybe eight washes.
I keep the Aster Marin Gloves in my jacket pocket year round. The Olive color goes with everything and they are thin enough to shift gears on my bike without taking them off.
The touchscreen fingertips on my Aster Marin Gloves stopped registering after about a month, which was half the reason I bought them. Fine as basic gloves, disappointing as tech gloves.
Ran a half marathon in freezing wind with the Aster Torrent Headband over my ears and forgot it was there by mile two. It covers the ears fully without that squeezing headache some bands give me.
I like the fleece lining but the Aster Torrent Headband creeps up the back of my head when I wear it over a ponytail. By the end of a run I have adjusted it four or five times.
The Aster Storm Scarf in Forest is my daily commute scarf now. It is soft against the neck, long enough to double wrap, and light enough that I do not overheat once I am on the train.
My Black Aster Storm Scarf sheds little fibers on everything it touches, especially lighter coats. Nice weight and drape, but I lint roll after every wear.
Bought the Aster Field Beanie in Sand for winter dog walks and it is genuinely warm without being bulky under a hood. The knit has held its shape through a couple of machine washes too.
It is a good beanie, but the Aster Field Beanie is 48 dollars and I am not sure the wool blend is that different from beanies half the price. Also runs a little snug if you have a bigger head.
The Aster Pace Cap has been on every summer run since May. It is so light I barely notice it, and the Navy has not faded even after weeks of sweat and sun.
Decent running cap, but sweat stains show badly on my Black Aster Pace Cap after hard workouts and they do not fully wash out. The brim is also floppier than I would like in wind.
For 20 bucks the Aster Boreal Socks punch above their price. The Oatmeal pair kept my feet warm through a whole day of snowshoeing and never got that clammy feeling.
A hole opened at the big toe of my Aster Boreal Socks within two months of normal wear. They are warm and the Sky color is nice, but the durability is just not there.
Surprised how much I use the Aster Alpine Gloves. Grabbed the Clay pair for early morning hikes and the grippy palms are great on trekking poles, plus 22 dollars felt fair.
Calling the Aster Alpine Gloves all-season is generous. They are fine in fall, but on a truly cold morning my fingertips went numb in about twenty minutes, and the White pair shows dirt instantly.
